data.title: TeamViewer: Worsening SMB ARR Growth Bad For The Sales Funnel
data.description:
TeamViewer (TMVWF) reported earnings, reversing the positive effect from a bit ago of the reiterated margin guidance for the FY 2025. The key negatives have been:

  • Margin compression people worried about for 2025 is still happening, but in 2026.
  • Leading indicators of ARR looking poor particularly in SMB which is the top of the TMV sales funnel. They are worsening a little bit in the middle-value deals for SMB compared to our previous coverage.

More generally, the company is contending with a tougher macro environment, more price sensitive SMB customers and also running afoul of AI-based trends such as possibly fewer seats in the per-seat model due to frictional unemployment risks and possibly a less valid thesis around AI possibly replacing TeamViewer and the related IT staff at corporations. FCFYs are closer to 20% at this point at between 180-200 million EUR in FCF against a 780 million EUR market cap. The valuation is compelling, but the SMB trends will mute the next two quarters of 2026 and could make performance of the stock difficult to achieve.

There were positives too this quarter like the conclusion of the launch of TeamViewer ONE (1E DEX and remote session offering combined) as well as some traction with Frontline (the AR offering) that had a notable impact on revenues. We don't see any structural dangers from AI materialising in the way markets may be worrying about for high margin lower-tech businesses like TeamViewer in actuality. Also a slight growth and margin hit still leaves the deleveraging angle intact with the 8 quarter target to return to more normal levels of leverage. That would be the angle we focus on.

Results

TeamViewer made a strategic mistake some quarters back trying to more aggressively monetise the SMB offering, irritating smaller users and landing badly. A tougher macro backdrop compounds the issue. Churns are rising. The issue is that SMB is the top of the funnel.

Negative ARR is really quite a problem because it's a leading indicator. This is going to be weighing on the entire 2026 outlook, where growth expectations are virtually flat despite expectations of decent upsell and traction of TeamViewer one among enterprise customers who will hopefully be eager to get their hands on the more comprehensive DEX-enhanced solution. Obviously, we hope that the autonomous endpoint management idea that was made possible by the 1E acquisition actually pans out.

Also, while margins were maintained as we discussed in our previous coverage for the FY 2025, the compression is getting pushed back into 2026 on weaker growth and also a block on price increases which can no longer land with the customer base, particularly with SMB where declining ARRs is not something the commercial pipeline can actually afford. It will take till the H2 2026 before sequential improvement resumes according to management phasing forecasts.

We do want to note that the growth overall in the current quarter was pretty good driven as always by enterprise. The re was a new deal signed for Frontline, adding possibly around ~5 million EUR in ARR in the Q4 (2% of ARR for enterprise). Sequential ARR growth did occur in Q4 driven by 1E picking up and some major European contract wins adding around 3 million to the ARR. But the NA market for 1E doesn't seem to be that great.

Bottom Line

Possible upside in 2026 could come from unexpected traction of the DEX solution in APAC where 1E was not present before. Also the roll-back of aggressive monetisation of the SMB client base could restore growth there faster than expected. The issue is that the negative exit rate from Q4 is worrying given that's a leading indicator, so there's an additional lag before it's going to be noticed positively in revenues.

But the reality is that the direction is worsening, and will likely stay underwhelming unless TeamViewer ONE gains unexpected traction, which we believe is unlikely given mounting macro pressure and generally low headcount additions. We don't see LLMs as a direct threat to TeamViewer other than the effect on headcounts in corporates in a seat-based model. In the end, IT departments need tools to interact with remote devices to fix problems. DEX and possibly more autonomous endpoint management still has value in an LLM world. We don't see this as a technological bet against AI. But with the direction not imminently improving the story is mainly about low absolute valuation reflected in around 20% FCFY and also the deleveraging angle.

Capital allocation priorities could thereafter change to something like a dividend which would be hefty on the current FCF generation and compressed price. We think the assets could be valued better once leverage ratios fall particularly in the face of lower growth rates, not a great combination but we don't see any major threats to cash generation with the company having made its future proof move already with 1E in our view.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PayPal Vs. Fiserv: Which Is The Better Comeback Bet?
data.description:
Intro

As many other analysts covering PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL), I have to admit that my positive call on the stock back in November hasn’t played out as expected so far. That said, the final assessment really needs to be viewed through a longer‑term lens, as I think the vast majority of ratings here on Seeking Alpha are given with a long‑term perspective in mind rather than short‑term swings. As the saying goes, in the short run the market is a voting machine, but in the long run it is a weighing machine (B. Graham). So, I believe some patience is good to have in such cases, while the short‑term dust settles and the longer‑term picture becomes clearer.

Still, since PayPal has seemed like one of the most convincing undervalued fundamental plays out there to me, I’m left wondering about the steep drop in its price—roughly one‑third—that investors have seen in recent weeks. With that in mind, I wanted to take a deeper look at what’s behind these latest wild moves in more detail. Whether this might be one of those rare, yet still occasionally occurring cases where the market overshoots so dramatically that the move itself ends up scaring potential buyers away, or if there’s something bigger going on beneath the surface.

Why PayPal Vs. Fiserv

While the headlines around PayPal’s stock cratering are nearly all pointing to challenges in the company’s “branded checkout” segment, a deeper dive suggests that the struggles aren’t solely the result of weak execution there—though I believe it could have been better—but also reflect a broader transitional shift within the payment‑processing landscape, or, if you prefer, the fintech sector more broadly.

To break down the situation, I started by listing what I see as the most significant and impactful recent changes in the payments space. As an investor exploring a diverse range of businesses across various industries, I can’t claim to be the ultimate fintech expert and may very well be missing some more nuanced developments, but here’s my take:

*Swipe payments.

*Apple Pay and other phone-linked payment services have rapidly become popular with customers, fueled by the widespread use of smartphones, tap-to-pay technology, and other related innovations.

*Instant payment services like FedNow and The Clearing House’s RTP in the U.S., and SEPA Instant in Europe, have sped up and simplified the process from merchant to buyer. This has put many “solution providers” under pressure, as the core account-to-account transfer is now so quick and easy that it often reduces or even eliminates the need for "middlemen" who once streamlined more complicated processes. Competition in transaction speed and ease of use has evolved from taking days to just seconds, and from manually entering pin codes to completing transactions with a smooth, effortless click or just a quick swipe.

*The rise of PayFac (PayPal's Braintree or PSP is an example of PayFac).

Explained in more detail by NMI:

PayFacs are companies that provide merchant services to businesses in various industries. Their primary service is payment processing – the ability to accept electronic payments via debit and credit card. But, many PayFacs also offer value-added services like fraud protection, secure data storage, advanced security (like tokenization) and more.

*BNPL has grown to the point where it’s now more like a normal part of everyday life, no longer just a kind of experiment in modern fintech.

*Young people are increasingly using services like Venmo, Cash App, Apple Cash, Chime, Revolut, and various crypto apps and wallets, thus often leaving traditional banks as a preferred option.

*The ever-changing crypto space is shifting toward stablecoins as a key driver in payment innovation.

While exploring changes in the payments industry and the key players involved, I kept coming across Fiserv, Inc. (FISV) — a leading name in the field. What also caught my eye was that the market has punished FISV, much like PayPal, with its stock plunging around 73%, making it the worst-performing publicly traded fintech company over a one-year period. PayPal, down 47%, ranks third from the bottom, almost on par with Shift4Payments (FOUR), which is down 51%.

Among other fintech peers, PayPal and Fiserv once again stand out with the lowest valuation multiples compared to close rivals like Block (XYZ), Global Payments (GPN), Fidelity National Information Services (FIS), Shift4 Payments (FOUR), and Adyen (ADYEY):

Both companies have recently undergone leadership changes, with headlines highlighting their struggles to keep up with the rapid pace of the evolving payments industry.

In 2019, Fiserv acquired First Data, a company that had been a trusted partner to banks since 1984. They are a global leader in their field and play a key role in banks' core processes. It seems like a very attractive business, as one might assume that, given the trust-based nature of banking, such deeply integrated partners aren’t easily replaced. Moreover, with the First Data takeover, Fiserv also got Clover, a leading name in the POS field, being one of the most innovative and fastest-growing players in its space.

In short, both PayPal and Fiserv started to look like highly appealing choices, with solid fundamentals that, if they got their act together, could make for a great turnaround play. So, I decided to pit them against each other to see which might hold the stronger hand in today’s fast-evolving payments landscape.

Competing and Collaborating "Pals"

PayPal's core headwind - struggling branded checkout isn’t the part of the business that directly competes with Fiserv. The area where they could go head-to-head is in payment processing, with PayPal's Braintree and Fiserv offering largely similar services. That said, as surprising as it seems, they are actually collaborating on cutting-edge stablecoin projects, according to a headline from June of last year.

Fiserv and PayPal Expand Partnership to Advance Stablecoin Payments

Excerpt from the press release:

“Businesses around the globe face common problems. They don’t receive funds fast enough, they are combating inflation, and many face currency value fluctuations. Blockchain-based technology solves many of these challenges by enabling faster transfers, at any time of day, nearly anywhere around the globe,” said Frank Keller, Executive Vice President at PayPal. “Together with Fiserv, we will help bridge the gap between traditional financial systems and new technologies, enabling faster, more cost-effective, and globally accessible payment options for merchants.”

So, potential competitors are shaking hands and exploring future payment innovations. It could be that they aren’t in intense competition, given such a friendly partnership. It’s a competitive industry that some players may be leaning more toward collaboration than trying to squeeze out every last penny. In this space, big names like FIS/Worldpay, Global Payments, Adyen, Stripe, and Jack Henry are all competing for their share.

To better understand the ins and outs of the payments ecosystem—from buyer to merchant—and the roles each party plays in routing buyers’ funds to merchants’ accounts, I think the following graph illustrates it pretty well:

Here, we have to remember that Fiserv owns FirstData, which means they play a key role in some cases, handling funds for both the buyer and the bank. With Clover added to the Fiserv model, which offers leading POS solutions to merchants (hardware+software), it could be said that Fiserv covers the entire process from buyer to merchant, except for the Payment Facilitators and Cards Networks segment. Still, they’re exposed to the latter through their card-issuing operations.

Although PayPal isn’t featured in this illustrative chain, its scope includes simple POS systems for small businesses under the Zettle brand (there might be some overlap with what Clover offers) on the merchant side and Payment Facilitators processing segment and payments processing unit through Braintree.

To sum up, while both companies operate in the same sector in general, their activities don’t seem significantly overlap. With their collaboration on blockchain developments, they really do seem more like a great fit for a win‑win partnership that can drive innovation across the entire payments value chain, just as they have announced.

Who Gets the Biggest Slice of the Payments Pie

The next graphs highlight which parts of the payments value chain take the biggest slice of the pie:

The largest share goes to the Issuing Bank and Processor, an area where Fiserv excels as a global leader. PayPal also secures a bigger cut when buyers pay using their PayPal balance, credit, BNPL, Venmo, or wallet. This is exactly why branded checkout is so important for PayPal, and when it struggles, it puts pressure on margins.

Risks and Challenges

PayPal

As just mentioned and explained in more detail for PayPal, while every part of their value chain is important and also supports other segments, the branded checkout is the so-called bread and butter.

Looking at PayPal's latest earnings release, while the headlines and the stock's some -20% drop after the announcement might suggest something is seriously broken, certain segments, like Venmo and PSP, have actually shown solid growth instead and even picked up momentum recently.

...but the summary shows that while Total payment volume (TPV) growth held steady at 6%, transaction margin excluding interest slowed to 4% last quarter, and non-GAAP EPS growth slipped to 3%:

These figures clearly show weakening margins, and as has been widely noted, one reason is that customers are using PayPal Branded Checkout less, with the graph indicating a 5% drop in transactions per active account.

Looking back at the broader industry-wide changes I outlined in the beginning, it’s clear that swipe payments—and especially Apple Pay or mobile payments in general—have shifted the dynamics in a way that puts PayPal at a disadvantage. Anyone who has paid with their phone knows how easy and smooth it is in most cases. This shift puts the control in the hands of such app-based wallet providers, with Apple Pay being the most dominant.

The issue for PayPal is that Apple Pay doesn’t allow payments directly from a PayPal account or wallet; instead, it requires a linked card. This means customers with PayPal accounts but no linked card to Apple Wallet or similar can’t choose to pay from their PayPal account, even though they could in other situations. I think this is a big reason why PayPal’s most profitable unit is struggling, and with how easy and seamless paying by phone is for buyers, it’s clearly here to stay, and PayPal needs to put a strong focus on finding ways to soften that largely unavoidable impact.

Fiserv

If PayPal's stock crashed after their latest earnings, Fiserv's largest crash happened in October last year. SA reports: Fiserv shakes up leadership, board after big Q3 earnings miss; stock plunges 30%

The latest earnings didn’t cause much movement in the stock, as the market had probably already factored in most of their challenges.

As clearly seen in their latest results, even though adjusted revenue is rising, margins and profitability are deteriorating at a significant pace:

...while Merchant Solutions figures are better, backed by Clover's strong dynamics, the challenges in Financial Solutions seem more worrisome:

After Q3, when shocking developments sent the stock down 30%, Eric Grover, principal at the consultancy Intrepid Ventures, summed up his perspectives on Fiserv well in my view:

“We’ve seen other massive payment processing consolidators like Worldline, FIS, and Global Payments, reverse course, looking to simplify,” Grover continues. “Fiserv competes with traditional processors and networks and a host of nimbler, newer players. It’s never going to act like a Silicon Valley startup. Nor should it. However, it needs a booster shot of dynamism.”

Side‑by‑Side Comparison and Valuation

PayPal Fiserv
The core problem The most profitable branded checkout is losing business.

Slow to adapt to rapid changes in fintech, competition has intensified and put significant pressure on margins. The Financial Solutions segment is particularly struggling.
2026 revenue growth roughly flat 1-3%
2027-2028 revenue growth 5% 4%
2026 EPS growth roughly flat -5%
2027-2028 EPS growth 10% 5%
EV/EBITDA 2026 5.9 7.1
Free Cash Flow 2026 $6 billion approx. $3.6 billion
Net Debt/Ebitda 0.3 3.1
Projected share repurchase $6 billon Projected, but a high net debt-to-EBITDA ratio could pose a risk
Mcap $36 billion $32 billion
Net Debt $3.5 billion $28.2 billion

Author's Creation

With PayPal’s enterprise value at $39.5 billion, giving it a forward 2026 EV/FCF multiple of 6.6, and Fiserv’s at $60.2 billion with a similar multiple of 16.7, I’m rating PayPal as a Buy and Fiserv as a Hold.

Takeaway

Both PayPal and Fiserv are facing challenges that squeeze their margins and put significant pressure on their growth prospects. At the same time, they each have strong growth drivers as well—PayPal’s Venmo and Fiserv’s Clover—growing more than 10%. With sharper execution, even if some struggling units continue to run roughly flat, the stronger parts of the business could still drive overall group‑level growth for both companies.

While I can’t predict which company will be easier to turnaround operationally, I clearly lean toward PayPal from a risk‑reward perspective. Its fundamentals look much stronger, whereas Fiserv is still weighed down by the heavy debt load it took on from the First Data acquisition.

I think it’s fitting well to wrap up with remarks from Fintechweekly, following Fiserv’s 30% stock plunge back in October last year. In my view, here’s a relevant summary for both companies:

Fiserv’s sharp correction may mark a difficult moment for the company, but it also represents a defining one — a reminder that in fintech, innovation and discipline must move in tandem if long-term trust is to be rebuilt.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Advance Auto Parts: Margins Impress Despite Muted Sales
data.description:
Shares of Advance Auto Parts (AAP) have been a volatile performer, but the stock is making progress recouping prior underperformance with shares up 30% over the past year. Investors received more good news Friday that the turnaround is on track, sending shares up 4% initially and back towards a 52-week high. I last covered shares in November, when I upgraded AAP to a hold from sell, given progress in advancing its turnaround. Frankly, I should have moved the stock all the way to a buy given the 25% run since then. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit Advance Auto Parts.

Margins impress as turnaround takes hold

In the company’s fourth quarter, Advance Auto Parts earned $0.86, blowing past estimates by $0.45. While revenue was down 1%, this was due to past store closings, and we saw same-store sales flip back positive, gaining 1.1%. Advance Auto Parts has taken significant action to simplify its business by selling assets, closing underperforming stores, and refocusing on the core business, improving inventory availability and reducing wait times. We are seeing this effort pay off. As you can see below, operating margins have improved substantially, and AAP is now solidly profitable. At the same time, this turnaround is far from over with margins still running well below management’s medium term target.

Drilling into Q4 results, gross margins were 44.2%, up 520bps last year. Last year’s margin was especially low given the blowout of inventory ahead of ~500 store closings and restructuring activity, but we are still seeing genuine margin expansion as it makes meaningful strides in better managing inventory. Even with slightly lower sales, adjusted gross profit was up nearly $100 million to $873 million.

This is an especially impressive performance considering the fact that tariffs have increased product costs to some degree, but AAP has been able to mitigate these pressures. Importantly, inventories appear to be in a healthy position at $3.7 billion, up just 1% from last year. AAP has limited total inventory growth while also improving in-store availability as it better aligns product-level inventory levels with demand. This has reduced the need to markdown product. Additionally, there are few things more frustrating than going to a store only for it to be out of stock, which can lead consumers to shop elsewhere. Solving this issue should help AAP gradually rebuild brand loyalty.

At the same time, I would note that sales growth is modest at ~1% on a same-store basis. To a large extent, I would describe AAP’s turnaround efforts as having stabilized the business. The company is making progress winning back pro customers, but do-it-yourself activity remains muted. Given ongoing affordability challenges, consumers are holding off on discretionary projects (i.e. paint jobs) and likely also deferring maintenance (ie waiting a few thousand miles more to change brakes). I expect this pressure to continue in 2026, limiting potential sales growth, though the pressure should not worsen relative to 2025.

At the same time, the company has done a very good job at removing excess costs, primarily by eliminating underperforming stores. As such, SG&A as a share of sales improved by 340bps to 40.5%. The company has reduced about 9% of its expenses, running at $800 million. AAP has reduced costs while also reducing wait times, again improving the customer experience.

Because of much better cost discipline, operating profits jumped to $73 million from a $99 million loss last year. Frankly, much of this improvement can be attributed to closing stores that were unprofitable. Its store count is down over 500 from last year. Rationalizing its store count has reduced sales cannibalization and allowed it to refocus on performance at remaining stores. We are now seeing the company inch back onto a growth footing. During Q4, it opened a net 8 locations. With sales growth so modest, I would like to see AAP move slowly on store openings to avoid repeating its prior problems.

Balance sheet supports the transformation

During 2025, it reported a $298 million free cash outflows due to nearly $500 million of working capital outflows as it needed to right-size account payables. I expect working capital to be less of a drag going forward. Looking at its balance sheet, the company is carrying $3.1 billion of cash. Against this, it has $3.4 billion of debt and $1.8 billion long-term operating leases. That results in net leverage ending the quarter at 2.4x.

Its balance sheet positioning reflects the fact its turnaround is still ongoing. Normally, a company would carry less gross cash and less gross debt. However, AAP likely has another $600+ million of cash costs associated with restructuring efforts between now and the end of 2026, which will consume some of its cash pile. Plus, past ratings downgrades pressured its vendor financing activities, contributing to a higher cash position. AAP pays a 1.7% dividend, which I view as secure, but these cash needs means buybacks are unlikely over the next 18 months.

Outlook is credible

Looking forward, management expects to earn $2.40-$3.10 on sales of just over $8.5 billion. It is important to be mindful that fiscal 2025 had 1 extra week, which is why guidance implies a modest sales decline. Guidance forecasts a 1-2% same-store sales increase adjusting for this. Its dividend costs about $61 million per year, so the company’s $100 million of free cash flow does cover this. It is encouraging to see AAP flip to being free cash flow positive.

AAP also plans to open over 50 new locations. This represents just about 1% growth in its footprint, so the company is not being aggressive about adding locations. As noted earlier, I do not think it should be as sales growth is modest. Indeed, I want to see management continue to focus on store productivity instead of growing its store count. Given residual tariff pull-through and general inflation, I expect prices to rise modestly, so 1-2% same-store sales growth likely implies little, if any, volume growth.

Guidance implies minimal sequential margin expansion from Q4 levels. While I think this is reasonable, this highlights the fact that AAP has already substantially reduced SG&A costs. To really accelerate margins from here, I believe we need to see increased top-line momentum. With consumer spending likely to grow just 1.5-2% this year in my view, 2026 is unlikely to witness such an acceleration. I continue to view its 7% medium-term target as highly ambitious and unlikely to be realized this year.

Shares are fairly valued

Given my view a recession is unlikely and seeing some upside to sales activity from new store openings relative to a fairly conservative guide, I see AAP earning $2.80-$3.20 this year, which leaves the stock right around 20x earnings, expensive for a slow-growth retailer. That said, full benefits of its ongoing transformation will not be apparent until next year. Back in October, I was targeting $4.50-$5 in 2027 EPS once the business is back to a more normalized run-rate position.

I still view this as a reasonable outlook, suggesting shares are ~12x 2027 earnings. That strikes me as a fair valuation, given ongoing execution risk and limited capital returns through 2027. AAP is not particularly expensive, especially as 2027 is more clearly coming into sight, so I would not be a seller here. However, we need to see an acceleration in sales growth to drive shares higher still, and in this macro backdrop, that seems unlikely. AAP is controlling its own performance well, but that appears reflected in valuation and I view shares as a hold.

data.title: Coinbase: Take Advantage Of Extreme Fear To 'Buy'
data.description:
From the outset, Bitcoin and crypto were never assets you should buy if you wanted to sleep soundly at night. Volatility has been paired to this asset class since its inception, and the jarring element about Bitcoin’s recent slide is that many first-time crypto investors, convinced by institutional participation and regulatory conversations, are experiencing their first major downward slide.

That said, it’s also too extreme to be overly optimistic and call a doomsday scenario for crypto, especially as Bitcoin appears to be finding support around the $65k range. Coinbase (COIN), the second largest crypto exchange and the largest wallet, looks like a particularly appealing "Buy" after a ~50% slide over the past year. While it’s certainly true that a full rebound in Coinbase will first require crypto market capitalization to recover, we also can’t ignore the opportunity to build a long-term position in this stock at its present decimated price.

I last wrote a "Buy" opinion on Coinbase in early January, when the stock was trading at $240. Needless to say, I didn’t foresee Bitcoin slipping through core price supports and dragging Coinbase down further. At the same time, however, I see many positive highlights coming out of Coinbase’s most recent Q4 earnings update (which prompted an initial post-earnings spike, though not nearly enough to combat losses in the trading sessions leading up to the earnings).

I’m reiterating my "Buy" opinion on Coinbase. The company is executing to the best of its ability as it navigates a difficult crypto market, and, importantly, it’s setting a strong foundation for profitable expansion when markets do rebound.

Buildup in Institutional Participation and Lower Transactional Costs

It’s true that at face value, Coinbase is facing a sharp decline in both revenue and profitability as consumers pull back from crypto trading out of fear. Individual investors’ trade volume still makes up the lion’s share of Coinbase’s haul.

That said, there are reasons for optimism too. One of the first bullish signals that I see is continued institutional support for Coinbase. Interestingly, Coinbase no longer reports trading volumes by customer type (consumer versus institutional) or by asset (Bitcoin, Ethereum, and other coins). But we can still see that while transactional revenue from consumers fell 46% y/y to $734 million, institutional trading revenue rose 31% y/y to $185.0 million.

Now, the growth in institutional volume is nowhere near enough to offset consumer declines. That said, the growth in institutional participation offers Coinbase an entirely different kind of exposure and stabilizes its business against retail traders. Many institutional investors are buy-and-hold players. We think that the sharp dip in Bitcoin may induce many asset managers to make room for crypto in their portfolios for the first time.

We note as well that Coinbase has done an excellent job recently at reducing its transactional expenses as a percentage of revenue. In Q4, transaction expenses hit a multi-quarter low at 13% of transactional revenue, down 1 point sequentially and down 1 point y/y.

The company is expecting further improvement in Q1'26, where guidance is calling for a low to mid-teens expense percentage (which we'll interpret to mean 13%-14%, or a 1-2 point reduction versus 15% in Q1'25).

Of course, Coinbase can't control the market and retail trading activity. But we like the fact that the company continues to build up its base of institutional investors while also gaining more margin per trade, setting the company up to be more structurally successful when the market rebounds.

Subscription and Services Revenue Momentum Continues to Build

The other point that we need to drive home is the fact that Coinbase's subscription and services revenue continues to represent an ever-increasing proportion of the overall business. In Q4, subscription & services revenue grew 13% y/y to $727 million, or 42.5% of Coinbase's total revenue - up 13 points from 29.2% in the year-ago quarter.

An annual view puts this into full perspective as well. In the past five years, Coinbase's non-trading revenue has quintupled. Note that this also compares back to the last peak of the Bitcoin cycle (when it hit ~$65k in November 2021). Bitcoin has fluctuated since then and is now back to similar levels, but Coinbase now has a substantially larger, more diversified stream of services revenue.

Revenue from stablecoins, of course, is a very critical catalyst for this growth. Despite falling interest rates, growth in USDC adoption has helped total stablecoin revenue in FY25 grow 48% y/y to $1.35 billion in FY25.

Note as well that USDC balances overall grew 12% sequentially in Q4 to $76 billion, while Coinbase's share of that balance grew ~20% sequentially to $18 billion.

Again, we emphasize the point here that despite crypto market/Bitcoin volatility, consumers' interest in USDC and stablecoins continues to rise. Steady income streams from stablecoins, on top of Coinbase Pro subscription revenues plus greater institutional participation in crypto markets, are all setting up Coinbase for success when the crypto market rebounds.

Valuation and Key Takeaways

There's no doubt that buying into Coinbase when the crypto market is swooning comes with a huge risk. That said, we also have a cheaper entry price to help take the edge off that risk. At current share prices near $140, Coinbase trades at a market cap of $38.05 billion. Netting off the $13.59 billion of cash, marketable securities, and crypto on Coinbase's recent balance sheet against $7.66 billion of debt gives us an enterprise value of $32.12 billion.

For FY26, Wall Street is expecting Coinbase to generate $8.17 billion in revenue, or 14% y/y growth. If we assume that Coinbase's revenue mix and its profit profile remain consistent with Q4'25 (when the company experienced the sharp pullback in retail trading), which clocked in at a 33% adjusted EBITDA margin (a degradation from the full-year FY25 adjusted EBITDA margin of 39%), then adjusted EBITDA against this revenue profile in FY26 would be $2.70 billion (-4% y/y).

This positions Coinbase's valuation at just 11.9x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA. To me, this is a tremendously low opportunity to buy Coinbase, especially as the company continues its pivot toward becoming the Everything Exchange and adding equities trading alongside prediction markets.

The risks in this stock (a sustained crypto slide) are very well known and pronounced in the share price. Yet I think Coinbase's opportunities (institutional growth, increased stablecoin/services revenue mix, improved transactional profitability, and diversifying revenue opportunities with stock trading) are being overlooked as the stock gets pummeled. Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Leggett & Platt: This Cigar Butt Doesn't Offer Enough Puffs (Downgrade)
data.description:
I believe many are familiar with the term Cigar Butt, which was one of the strategies Warren Buffett used in the past, but I will explain it briefly for those who are not familiar with it. Basically, it is an analogy to a cigar that still has a few final puffs left and is not yet at the end, that is, referring to extremely cheap companies that no one wants but that still have some potential to provide a return.

Leggett & Platt, Incorporated (LEG) fits this description somewhat (not to mention the potential of the M&A). It is a company with very limited financials and very challenging prospects, but it still manages to generate some cash from operations, and as the stock is cheap, it can be understood that in the coming years there may be some high level of shareholder yield.

But even so, there are so many uncertainties that I don't think the valuation still compensates for this declining business.

Leggett's Q4 Earnings: Far From Good

Legget’s Q4 earnings were not surprising to the market, but still, there is no other way to describe it than another weak quarter. Revenue was in line with consensus estimates, and EPS was a miss by “only” $0.01. As for 2026's outlook, Leggett indicated a top line between $3.8 and $4 billion and EPS between $1 and $1.20, both very close to the consensus.

To me, it is not so interesting when combined with other factors from the quarter. Sales was down 11% YoY. Well, this is not so worrying when we consider that Leggett & Platt is in a transition phase, having made several divestments and similar moves. The problem is that the impact of divestitures was -5%, while volume fell 9%, i.e., it was a quarter heavily impacted by demand and product volume, not just adjustments. The EBIT margin remained stable.

When compared to the full year, this quarter was much worse than average. In 2025, revenue fell 7%, impacted by a 6% decline. The full-year margin was also a little better than in Q4 alone. So, although the other quarters were not so good either, Q4 earnings were worse.

The good news is, Leggett’s management is really committed to the initiatives that have been outlined in recent quarters. For instance, there were actually divestitures, and as a result, Leggett & Platt was able to reduce its net debt from $1.5 billion at the end of 2024 to $900 million, so even with a certain decline in adjusted EBITDA, leverage fell from 3.8x to 2.4x (net debt/EBITDA).

Also, costs have actually fallen, and the EBIT benefit was even better than expected, but sales attrition was slightly worse, i.e., LEG’s management is not only talking and making plans but is also delivering some quite reasonable results.

Leggett & Platt: Tough Future

I’m still not that optimistic because it seems increasingly difficult to envision a truly healthy future for Leggett & Platt, and especially to be convinced that this will happen. What I mean here is that, for 2026, management sees another decline in top line and EPS. Okay, it's part of the game; they are capturing efficiencies and optimizing the portfolio, but my point is, what's next? And in 2027-2030? I cannot be convinced that Leggett & Platt will be a company that can deliver consistent results. I mean, solid revenue growth in the next decade, healthy margins, and consequently a return to generating attractive shareholder value, unlocking upside through re-rating, and so on.

For instance, they mentioned in the earnings call that the residential market is not doing well and that when it turns around, they can capture that growth, but that also seems very intangible to me. I understand that interest rates are high, the real estate market is far from booming, and that an improvement in this cycle could benefit Leggett & Platt.

But in my view, these negative effects are not explained only by external factors, but also by competition, product innovation, and that Leggett & Platt has gradually fallen behind for a number of reasons.

This also shows how difficult this industry is. Even with a brand name, in bad economic cycles, consumers will give up more premium brands for other alternatives, while consumers who still have money may seek out even more innovative/premium products.

In any case, speaking of financials, I still find it difficult to be convinced that Leggett & Platt will have healthy, growing cash flow in the coming years. It is still unclear, and the company needs to continue delivering and going through these transition phases.

Valuation: Cigar Butt? Maybe

As for the leverage I mentioned earlier, 2.4x is not exactly bad, but we have to remember that Leggett's financials for the coming years are still uncertain, so it is not the “optimal” level that allows flexibility and for the company to spend the cash it has.

In other words, all this cash flow yield that can turn into buyback yield and/or dividend yield, in my view, it might even be better if there were not such a high payout so that this balance sheet remains robust, and the company can invest more to improve its prospects.

But in any case, note that the yield would still not be that high. Using the bottom of the guidance, we are talking about an operating cash flow of $225 million and a CapEx of ~$100 million, meaning there is $125 million left over for shareholders (this disregards debt, in case the company wants to reduce leverage). And the market cap today is $1.5 billion. In other words, this free cash flow yield is less than 10%, which seems too low to treat this case as “highly asymmetric” or “deep value.”

I believe that the only move left is M&A. Somnigroup (SGI) recently made an offer of $12 per LEG share, but it was rejected. Personally, considering the current multiples, it didn't seem like such an unusual offer to me as Leggett & Platt made it seem by saying that “The Board determined that Somnigroup's $12 per share proposal undervalues the Company and declined the proposal.”

The good part is that even without much information, the companies are still in talks, and perhaps this deal will continue to move forward to unlock upside for shareholders. Somnigroup is not just looking at cash flow and debt, so it may make sense to pay a little more for the brand and other assets. But even so, the P/B is at ~1.5x with LEG stock around $11.7, so I also don't see a scenario where the proposal changes dramatically.

Bottom Line

M&A is part of the financial market game. In fact, I made good gains with Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) due to the war of deals we saw recently, but I chose to exit early. Personally, I don't really like to depend on deals being made by a third party and then approved by the board, shareholders, and, in some cases, other regulatory bodies.

Obviously, if the Leggett & Platt case still looked very attractive, I would view the stock as a hold, i.e., if the expected cash flow yield were very high or if the current multiples were so low that a new offer from Somnigroup could unlock much more upside (let's say, if it bought at 20x earnings, that would be more than 100% upside, and that does not seem to be the case). Therefore, as the stock has continued to rise since my last article, with a total return of ~26%, even with a still very unclear scenario, and seems to have already unlocked most of the upside that could be achieved (in the short term), I am downgrading it to a Sell, signaling that I prefer to avoid this stock and watch this story from afar.

data.title: Nebius: Incrementally Bullish After Positive Report
data.description:
Nebius Group (NBIS) has quickly transformed into one of the most talked about - and bullish - stocks on Seeking Alpha. Investors have consistently been looking for stocks with direct exposure to the AI infrastructure build, and NBIS has demonstrated they are a leading AI hyperscaler.

My thesis has evolved over the past year, moving from bullish when the stock was in the $70s to bearish when excessive optimism pushed the stock to $130+. However, I upgraded the stock at the start of the year and feel even more comfortable in a bullish thesis after the most recent quarterly report.

Of course, there will still be a lot of skeptics on the infrastructure build-out and the ability for NBIS to achieve some of its stated financial targets. However, the market so far in 2026 has made one thing very clear: investors want exposure to AI.

As discussed more below, the company provided some bullish, yet achievable, financial targets for 2026. The company is pivoting from a leasing-based model to a more self-developed and full-stack approach to data centers. At face value, this should improve total cost of ownership and technical control over the entirety of the process, but added risks around capital intensity.

Although the stock has been in a bit of a holding period so far this year, it has outperformed the pure software companies and general tech indices. For now, I remain very bullish about NBIS' opportunity to expand its operations and achieve its financial targets.

Financial Update

When looking at the actual Q4 results, it might show a bit of a mixed signal on-demand trends. Revenue grew an impressive 547% yoy to $228 million, though it was noticeably below expectations for $243 million. Adjusted net loss of $173 million was much worse than the $69 million loss in the year-ago quarter. Yet, these near-term results are not the most important factor.

Revenue recognition is very important for NBIS, as getting capacity only is a significant determinant in when revenue can be recorded. I have discussed this more in depth in one of my prior articles.

Depending on when capacity comes online determines when NBIS is able to recognize revenue. So if there are any delays in building out the data centers and getting the consumption power available, this would be a direct headwind to revenue. As long as the underlying contract is not canceled or down-sold, then it's not a major issue. However, if delays become more consistent, this could ultimately end up being a constant revenue pressure and potentially call into question the ability of NBIS to deliver on its contracts.

Rather, the more important metrics for NBIS are around its active and contracted power.

Active power is what is actually being consumed by the IT equipment. This is when NBIS can start generating revenue. At year-end, the company had 170 MW of active power, well above the prior expectations of 100 MW. Thus, NBIS was able to deliver its capacity ahead of schedule - Positive #1.

Contracted power is the amount of power secured by contractual agreements. Though not revenue, this is a great representation of what's yet to come. NBIS announced 9 new sites across the US and EMEA regions, bringing the expected contracted power to 3 GW, up from the prior target of >2.5 GW. A majority of these new sites will be delivered through NBIS' owned data centers, rather than the company's prior strategy of leasing data centers.

One big area of focus for 2026 will be progress towards the 800 MW to 1 GW of connected power. This is somewhat of an ambitious feat, as it requires crisp deployment execution, which is challenging in a supply-chain-constrained environment. If any issues arise with the data center inputs or construction, the connected power targets would be at risk.

During the earnings call, management provided some additional commentary on their supply chain resilience.

We already contracted the majority of our long-lead items around our own sites to ensure the capacity development beyond 2026 as well.

The first important point is our largest deals with Microsoft and Meta: we were able to secure the necessary components last year for the full scope of those contracts, and we secured them in the second half of 2025 before any price increase.

For the remaining, we are confident in our supply chain to get the parts we need to continue to deliver the capacity.

Certainly a positive to see management confident in its supply chain. But it is important to note that input costs, such as chips and memory, have become exponentially more expensive in recent months. Yes, some of the contracts already signed might be at more favorable supply chain prices, but the newer contracts are likely to be more expensive to deliver.

For 2026, the company reiterated its target of achieving $7-$9 billion of ARR, which is not dependent on new mega-deals, such as the ones signed with Microsoft and Meta. Both of these contracts remain on schedule, with Meta capacity being deployed in early February 2026 and Microsoft's first tranche delivered on time in November 2025, with remaining tranches to be deployed throughout 2026.

One final issue that may need to be addressed in 2026 is potential debt and/or equity financing. With NBIS providing Capex guidance of $16-$20 billion for the year, they will likely need to find some source of funds. This is essential Capex as it involves building out the data centers to deliver on the contracted power.

The company's balance sheet is healthy with ~$3.7 billion in cash and ~$4.0 billion of debt. NBIS also has an at-the-market equity program that they can tap into for funds (not used to date) as well as equity stakes in ClickHouse and Avride that can be future sources of capital.

Valuation

Valuation has often been the main deterrent for investors when it comes to these AI names. The end-market exposure, revenue growth potential, and leadership position all warrant a premium valuation, in my opinion. As mentioned above, there is some timing issue when it comes to revenue recognition, so a forward revenue multiple is not 100% clean.

However, many have looked at the company's ARR as a proxy.

Right now, NBIS trades at ~6.3x forward revenue. However, if we were to look at the guided $7-$9 billion of ARR by the end of 2026, then the valuation is even more attractive at just ~3x. Of course, ARR is not revenue, and there is a material ramp in ARR for NBIS this year.

That being said, the company's mega-contracts with Meta and Microsoft are proceeding as scheduled, and this should give investors increased confidence in achieving the ARR target.

Of course, there are some additional risks to consider, but given the momentum NBIS has displayed in recent quarters and the company's pivot to own the full-stack, I am very bullish at these levels.

Risks

Ultimately, the biggest risk centers around the Meta and Microsoft contracts. If either of these are impaired—whether it's due to supply chain constraints, delayed project deadlines, or other factors - the stock would have material downside. Additionally, as the hyperscalers gain greater visibility into their own training and inference demands, there is always the potential for these companies to move work in-house.

Lack of profitability is also a risk. Yes, the company guided adjusted EBITDA margin of 40% for 2026, but this is largely D&A add-backs. Adjusted EBIT is expected to remain a loss in 2026, which implies negative FCF. If NBIS is not able to show increased profitability as the company scales, this could cap the valuation multiple.

Finally, the massive $16-$20 billion in Capex requirements for 2026 might trigger the need to raise capital. Management is confident they can do this efficiently, but this might come at the expense of large debt loads or equity issuance, which would dilute current shareholders.

Final Thoughts

NBIS has once again demonstrated that its leadership position as an AI hyperscaler is gaining even more traction in the market. The company's continued build-out of infrastructure combined with taking a more proactive full-stack ownership approach should yield material benefits down the road.

As long as NBIS marches towards its ARR target while efficiently raising capital to support the Capex, I believe the stock is a multi-year winner at these levels.

data.title: The $31 Trillion Elephant In The Room
data.description:
Written by Sam Kovacs

Introduction

I have a recurring nightmare.

It's not a monster under the bed.

It's an elephant in the room, called the US fiscal deficit.

Let's start with the blunt truth: The United States government is running the largest peacetime deficit in its history, all the while its economy is growing, unemployment is manageable, and there is no crisis demanding emergency spending.

Every single period of comparable deficit was associated with war, financial crises, or pandemics.

Deficits that were once emergency only have now morphed into our baseline.

This article will give an overview of where we stand, the path forward, what needs to be true for debt to not be a problem, and consequences that would arise from more likely paths.

If you know in your bones that something isn't quite right but haven't thought through all of the ramifications, this article is for you.

The current fiscal situation:

The Congressional Budget Office has released its February 2026 Budget and Economic Outlook, which gives us the most current picture available.

Here's what you need to know to understand the severity of the situation:

  • The government is spending $970bn per year just to service debt.
  • That is equivalent to $7,300 per household per year.
  • That's more than households spend annually on healthcare ($6,500), gas ($2,000), clothing ($2,200), or education ($1,200)
  • The cost of servicing interest is growing by 10% per year.
  • Net interest is the 3rd biggest expenditure.
  • Only Social Security and Medicare cost more. Defense costs less than interest on the debt...
  • In 5 years, it is projected to pass Medicare also.

Just 20 years ago, US public debt stood at $4.6tn.

It has 6x'd since. That's a growth rate just shy of 10% per year, while nominal GDP has grown at 4.4% per year.

Debt has gone from 60% to 120% of GDP.

(If we consider debt held by the public, we're at like 99-100%. Important to not get confused later.)

We're at a point where causes are just compounding one on top of the other:

  • Fiscal stimulus and bank bailouts during the 2008 crisis.
  • Nearly $6tn in pandemic spending.
  • Repeated tax cuts with no offsetting spending reductions (2001, 2003, 2017, and now again 2025 with the OBBB)
  • And from 2022, a sharp rise in interest rates, which changed the math on servicing debt.

The most gut wrenching truth behind it all, is that we are now within a self-reinforcing debt spiral.

More debt means more interest, which contributes to more deficits, which means more borrowing, which means ever more debt.

The interest cost of the government doubled from 2022 to 2025.

They're projecting it could double again by 2035...

Which begs the question... what's the path forward?

The path forward in 3 scenarios.

I like to think in probabilities. I try and draw out chain-of-event style scenarios in my mind, and ask myself what needs to be true for this to be the case, and come up with some alternative paths for reality.

This gives me the flexibility to switch to another scenario when the data starts pointing one way rather than another.

I'll map out 3 scenarios here: The first is the CBO's baseline path. The second is an accelerated path, and the third is the scenario the current Administration is providing, which is the optimistic case.

Scenario 1: Baseline CBO

This is the baseline path given by the CBO, which assumes that all current laws remain in effect, with current tariff levels and scheduled expirations of anything temporary.

It shows debt held by the public relative to GDP rather than total debt.

If we stay on this path, debt will grow from $31tn today to $56tn in 2036. We'd see the ratio of debt held by the public to GDP surpass the World War II record.

Deficits would never fall below 5.5% of the GDP, which would be double what Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent is stating as a 3% target.

In this scenario, the OBBB adds $4.7tn in debt, partially offset by $3tn in tariffs.

The problem I have with this scenario, of course, is that it assumes a quite uniform projection, as seen below.

If anything can be learned by looking at the chart above, it is that we shouldn't expect smooth sailing. Turbulence, shocks, and unexpected events will very certainly happen between today and 2036.

Scenario 2: Accelerated deficit path

This path is the one that most fiscal hawks are currently viewing as the true trajectory.

It accounts for the near certain likelihood of temporary provisions being made permanent, just as the 2017 tax cuts were extended.

The Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget provided this scenario path in August 2025.

Under this scenario, all temporary OBBB measures are made permanent. The current trade court ruling that tariffs are illegal is upheld, and treasury yields remain at current levels rather than declining.

Here debt would reach 134% of GDP in 2036.

10 years from now, interest costs alone could be consuming 5% of GDP... let that scenario sink in.

Scenario 3: The Administration's projections

If we can offer a hawkish alternative, we can also offer a dovish one.

The current administration's council of economic advisors has offered a very different outcome.

(They assume that real GDP grows at 4% per year throughout 2028, and then at 2% thereafter. This is a big step up from the CBO's 2.2% growth to 2026, then 1.8% thereafter).

They project debt to fall to 97% of GDP by the end of 2026, and to 94% by 2034.

Their math conveniently solves the debt problem by growth.

So the current stance of the administration, the current disregard for the deficit, and the widening deficit that is yet to come, is to say: "don't worry, growth is coming".

Basically banking on an AI miracle.

But it does beg the question. If Bessent, Warsh, Trump, and the current administration are to be right, what needs to be true?

What needs to be true for debt not to be a problem?

Above, I presented three scenarios. In two of them, debt becomes an increasing problem.

So let's consider the third path. If debt is to stabilize or even improve relative to GDP without violent disruptions, what set of conditions needs to materialize?

Below are 5 conditions that need to hold true for the math to work out.

Not all need to be true, but if we don't get at least a few of them, then the math just doesn't work.

Condition 1: GDP grows at 3%+ per year

In a debt/GDP equation, the denominator does matter.

If we can get the economy to grow fast enough, the ratio can stabilize even with persistent deficits.

The central point of the administration's entire framework is that they can grow by 4 per annum through 2028.

The 50 year average real GDP growth rate is about 2.6%.

We effectively need an AI miracle to make this happen. And while the Capex cycle is well and alive, we haven't yet seen broad productivity gains spread throughout the economic productivity data.

Basically, the math is such that with the current labor force 0.5% growth rate, productivity growth would need to double to 3% per year sustainably.

Now I do see how AI could deliver this eventually; it's quite unclear what the timeline is of this clearly showing up in the data.

Currently, the probability of this being true seems quite low, as it depends on speculative impacts of AI on productivity, which would surpass the measurable gains from the internet or the smartphone, and do so sustainably, rather than a one time level change.

I estimate that there is maybe a 15-20% chance that this condition can be true between now and the end of the decade.

Condition 2: Interest rates fall materially and stay low

If we can get treasury yields to decline enough, the interest burden becomes way more manageable.

If we can somehow achieve a flat-ish yield curve at sub 3%, then we'd be able to reduce the interest burden significantly.

Right now this seems very unlikely. We'd need to see some combination of: recession, deflation, or global demand for safe haven assets.

This flies in the face of the whole debt spiral argument, where more debt leads to higher rates on that debt.

If yields end up getting pinned, well, that just fuels the deficit.

Once again, the only hope is an "AI miracle" to drive deflation hard in the next two years.

If we don't see headline inflation going below 2% anytime soon, this argument gets difficult to believe.

Yes, they'll slam the front end of the curve. That will drop the whole curve somewhat, but then how do they manage the long end of the curve? It's not clear that they can control it. At least not without further increasing the deficit, which they might do.

I assign a 10-15% likelihood of this condition.

Condition 3: Tariff revenue materializes as projected.

The current CBO baseline projection relies on $3tn of debt reduction from tariffs over the next decade.

The US trade court has ruled that much of the tariff framework is illegal. It's up to the Supreme Court now to decide whether this is upheld.

A big part of the justification of OBBB is that tariffs will be upheld and generate large amounts.

Here I assign a 50% likelihood of the condition holding. It's a bit of a coin toss at this point, and I really don't have insight to offer.

I do think that if the tariff revenues disappear due to a ruling, the OBBB likely still stays.

Condition 4: Congress demonstrates fiscal discipline

Please don't laugh.

If Congress could reduce spending equivalent to 2.9% of GDP per year, then the debt to GDP level would be stabilized.

This is the same as cutting spending equivalent to 1/3rd of income tax revenues.

Let's not kid ourselves, the probability of this is maybe 1-2%.

There is no political constituency for austerity. One side of the aisle won't tolerate tax hikes, the other won't tolerate cuts to benefits.

An aging population does not help.

The midterm elections in November only add pressure for spending commitments. There will be no restraint.

Condition 5: The dollar retains reserve currency status

The reason the US can borrow at relatively low rates despite its very high debt is because of its status as the global reserve currency.

This creates a structural demand floor for US government debt that no other country can lay a claim to.

Take this away, and the math just doesn't work.

I believe that over the next decade there is a very good chance that we see this stay true. There is no viable alternative today.

There are some cracks under the surface, with China encouraging banks to reduce treasury holdings, and central banks worldwide moving back to gold as a reserve.

The risk, I think, is more a gradual repricing of the dollar privilege, which has allowed the US to borrow cheaply.

Probability of the US retaining reserve currency status: 85-90%.

Synthesizing the most likely path.

If we consider the fickle conditions needed to reach the miracle path, it becomes difficult to see it as anything better than a 10% chance of success.

That's actually how I'd do the math now: 40% scenario 1, 50% scenario 2, 10% scenario 3.

This means that we are very likely facing a situation where the deficit becomes a growing problem, and its costs to society and the US increase dramatically.

You should understand that I am praying for scenario 3 to be true, as it would lead to the best outcomes for us all.

If AI is going to save us, it doesn't have much time to start doing it.

Now let's consider some consequences of this most likely path of more debt and more deficits.

Consequences to reckon with

Consequence 1: The interest cost death spiral

This is the most certain consequence. It's mathematical, mechanical, and unlike in Hollywood movies, we cannot suspend reality; we have to face it head on.

If we are not saved by AI, interest costs will more than double in the upcoming decade.

More than one in five dollars collected in tax goes into debt servicing, which produces no infrastructure, no public services, no defense, and no benefits.

To maintain current expenditure, you have to increase the deficit just to accommodate the debt servicing. This compounds over time and only gets worse.

Consequence 2: Crowding out of public and private investment

This massive deficit needs to be financed by those that have capital. As the government absorbs an ever larger share of the world's available savings, there is less capital available for private investments.

This would slow economic growth, as it reduces capital formation, research and development, and infrastructure that could later generate growth.

This puts a massive headwind on corporate America.

Consequence 3: Reduced wiggle room in crisis.

This is maybe the most dangerous of all of the consequences exposed here, but when the next big crisis happens, what will the response from the federal government be?

Will they shamelessly inject trillions in emergency spending?

Does the market have the same appetite for these decisions when interest expenses are going through the roof?

In such an environment you get no good solutions. Emergency spending is fuel on the fire. Letting the fire burn is not good either.

Consequence 4: Bond market volatility and yield repricing

As the debt supply grows with no end in sight, the market has to absorb these treasuries as we saw above.

If there is not the appetite to hold these, then the yield on these bonds must go up simply for the market to clear.

This means that the yield curve would look higher than it does today, as I don't believe that we are fully pricing this fiscal endgame scenario that we are facing.

This would shock equity multiples, increase mortgage rates, raise corporate borrowing costs, and slow economic growth.

Consequence 5: Inflationary knock-on effects

Large deficits create demand that is stolen from the future. It keeps inflationary pressure, and this constrains the Fed.

The Fed just cannot cut rates as aggressively if fiscal stimulus keeps the economy running hot.

Now, to be clear, with the current narrative, it seems that they will slam rates and disregard short term inflation, which is potentially very dangerous.

The structural concern is if markets catch on to the idea that the long term escape from the debt spiral is to inflate it away, by letting inflation exceed interest rates, to erode the real value of the debt.

This would be bad and would unanchor inflation expectations and lead to higher yields and worse credibility.

This happened to the UK Gilts in 2022, when unfunded fiscal expansion caused a bond market rout.

Consequence 6: Public trust fund insolvency

Both Social Security and Medicare trust funds are approaching insolvency on accelerated timelines.

Once the reserves from prior decades are exhausted, there are only two ways to deal with the program: cut benefits (politically catastrophic) or finance them through additional borrowings.

Mandatory spending obligations are growing faster than the economy is growing.

At the same time, the debt needed to finance the mandatory spending is growing faster than the economy.

What will break the system

The sobering takeaway of this analysis is that we are looking down the line and staring at a sovereign debt crisis.

The question then becomes one of focusing on catalysts that force a reckoning, and potential timelines.

Trigger 1: A failed Treasury auction

This would be particularly dramatic, but seems very unlikely currently. If a major auction doesn't see demand follow through, the Treasury has to accelerate significantly higher yields, this would crash risk assets overnight.

This has never happened, but never doesn't mean it cannot happen. The gradual reduction in global central bank demand, notably from China, all while the future size of issuance grows, means that the probability of this down the line increases.

Currently I'd say 2-5% occurrence in the next couple of years.

Trigger 2: Further credit downgrades

The US was first downgraded by S&P in 2011, and Fitch and Moody's in 2023. Any further downgrades would force institutions to shuffle for their next move and amplify sell-offs in treasuries and derisking from holders abroad.

I rate this at maybe a 15-20% chance. The rating agencies are often reactive rather than proactive, so I wouldn't put too much weight on it.

Trigger 3: A sudden shift in inflation expectations

If the market starts pricing fiscal monetization (the idea that the Fed will be forced to buy government debt to keep yields manageable), then inflation expectations would spike.

This would give the Fed a horrible choice: support the bond market, or fight inflation.

This is a situation that scares me, because it is increasingly obvious to me that some form of yield curve control down the line is the most likely outcome for the US.

Growth needs to follow through, or at least the narrative of it for long enough, for inflation expectations to not unanchor.

I think it's maybe 1 in 4 odds?

Trigger 4: A geopolitical shock that tests Dollar hegemony

If a major geopolitical shock accelerates a shift away from the dollar as the global hegemony, then we can expect everything to spiral much faster.

China's recent decision to incentivize its banks to reduce Treasury exposure is an early warning sign. If other central banks follow, it could be fast trouble.

Like I said, this isn't the most likely to me, but it's possible.

1 in 10?

Trigger 5: a slow burn.

This is maybe the most likely outcome. A slow, grinding deterioration in conditions.

Premiums on treasuries rise gradually, interest costs crowd out spending, equity multiples compress, and the dollar weakens. Living standards improve more slowly. Inequality rises.

Investment considerations

For equity investors, the fiscal trajectory is a secular headwind to P/E multiples in the long run.

In the short run however, especially if the economy is left to run hot as it seems is the plan, equities will do well.

In particular, real world businesses. Those with cash flows and profits, not growth promises for the future.

The discount rates of certain sectors can be repriced in weeks and give investors a haircut which doesn't recover for years.

We continue to favor stable dividend stocks, and lean towards small caps with pricing power and niche moats in the current environment.

The honest assessment of the whole situation is that whatever the next big market shock is, the fiscal backdrop makes clawing our way out so much more difficult.

It's a time to be conservative, but it doesn't mean that a crisis is just around the corner.

The US retains magnificent advantages, and if AI saves us all, the US will be the prime beneficiary.

But we've basically put it all on the line, for what right now looks like a long term 10% probability of success.

That's the elephant in the room. The elephant is getting bigger, but the room isn't.

data.title: Twilio: Buy These Strong Results
data.description:
After the bell on Thursday, we received fourth quarter results from Twilio (TWLO). The customer engagement platform has been a nice growth story in recent years, and the company has finally started to deliver some solid GAAP operating profits. While a number of software and services names have been under pressure recently, Twilio's solid quarter and guidance could set the stock up for a strong 2026.

Previous coverage of the name

It was about six months ago when I last covered the stock, right after Twilio had reported its second quarter results for 2025. Revenues came in nicely ahead of estimates, while bottom line improvement continued. With management providing its usual conservative guidance, investors weren't too thrilled initially. I thought that provided an opportunity, and since then, the stock rallied more than 11.5% (to Thursday's close), compared to a 6.9% rise in the S&P 500, despite a recent pullback.

The Q4 earnings report

For the final period of 2025, revenues came in at $1.37 billion, up 14% on a reported basis and 12% in terms of organic growth, with pass-through revenue from incremental U.S. carrier fees providing a boost as detailed on the Q3 earnings call. Both numbers, however, were still well ahead of the 10.2% growth that analysts were looking for. Twilio has not missed top line estimates in the past five years, and each of the beats in last year's quarters were at least $33 million.

The company reported over 402,000 active customer accounts at the end of 2025, up from over 325,000 a year earlier. The dollar-based net expansion rate was 109% for Q4 2025, up three percentage points year over year and seven points in the last two years. The strong results were described as following by Khozema Shipchandler, CEO of Twilio:

2025 was one of the most balanced and successful years of execution in Twilio’s history and has fundamentally transformed our financial profile and innovation velocity.

We accelerated revenue growth, expanded operating margins, and delivered significant growth in free cash flow. Importantly, our vision is resonating with customers and Twilio is quickly becoming a foundational infrastructure layer in the age of AI.

The area where Twilio has been most impressive lately though comes further down the income statement. For Q4 2025, GAAP operating income came in at almost $57 million, several times the nearly $14 million seen a year earlier. Adjusted operating income also rose from $197 million to more than $255 million. The company did report a larger GAAP loss than last year due to some equity investment impairments, but the adjusted profit rose from $1.00 to $1.33 per share, beating the street by a dime. This was the fourth straight non-GAAP bottom line beat, with only one miss in the last five years.

Balance sheet and guidance update

Twilio finished last year with $2.47 billion of cash and short term investments, while also holding roughly $1 billion in long term debt. That cash position was up about $90 million for the year. The company generated over $945 million of free cash flow, a 19% margin, up significantly from $658 million and 15%, respectively, for all of 2024. The majority of that cash flow went to share repurchases, mostly to offset stock based compensation, but also to help reduce the share count a little.

For the current quarter, management is calling for revenue growth in a range of 14% to 15% on a reported basis and 10% to 11% on an organic one. Both ranges were ahead of the 9.7% growth the street was looking for. For the year, reported growth is expected to be 11.5% to 12.5%, with organic at 8% to 9%. The street was looking for 8.7%, so this guidance is fair, especially when you consider management is usually conservative. I expect we'll see revenue guidance and perhaps the adjusted profit/cash flow forecast raised during the year. Management is calling for free cash flow to accelerate to at least $1.04 billion, which I expect will go mostly to the buyback again.

The current valuation picture

Recently, a lot of names tied to the Artificial Intelligence revolution have been hit due to concerns over growth and valuation. Twilio on Thursday basically showed that it is growing in line with or even above expectations. The stock finished Thursday trading at just over 20 times expected adjusted earnings for the year and just over 3 times expected sales. Many of the AI high flyers go for well above that on an adjusted P/E basis and in the high single digits or even low double digits on a price to sales basis. Twilio may not be growing as fast as those names currently, but you're paying a much more reasonable price.

As for the street, analysts are quite positive on the stock. Going into Thursday's report, there were 29 analysts who had rated the stock in the past three months, of which 18 had some kind of buy rating and 9 had holds, with just 2 sells. The average price target was over $145, which now implies well over 30% upside from current levels. That average is up $12 since I last covered the name and well above the mid-$60s area where that average target bottomed a few years ago.

Final thoughts and recommendation

Twilio reported another strong set of results on Thursday, handily beating street estimates on the top and bottom lines. The company's revenue growth has accelerated in recent years, even on an organic basis, and customer metrics all look very good. As the name has now shifted to GAAP profitability, cash flow has soared, and the buyback has brought the share count down nicely over time. This year's guidance was solid given management's usual conservatism, which should set up the company for a strong year.

Given the latest set of results, I'm continuing to rate Twilio as a buy. The company is in a much better place than it was just a few years ago, and further revenue growth should lead to even better bottom line and cash flow results as well. The company is in a good spot to take advantage of the AI revolution, and the valuation is very fair compared to many tech peers. If the overall market can calm down a bit, I think this is a nice name to own in 2026.

data.title: Oscar Health: Macro Headwinds Hurt In 2025 Are Expected; Eyes On Massive Price Discovery In 2026
data.description:
Post-earnings investment thesis

Oscar Health (OSCR) is a health insurance company which leverages technology to enhance operational efficiency. While our investment thesis and conviction in the Company had been explained in previous analysis, the key words I would like to elaborate here are health insurance and technology.

They are key because investors who do not understand thoroughly what the Company does will not understand the value it has. And the most recent earnings announcement has reiterated the importance of the understanding.

Health insurance: Government-level headwinds offset by immense individual-level demand tailwinds

Macro health insurance has been under pressure for the broader industry, ever since President Donald Trump's second term in which he starts rolling back insurance packages such as Medicaid and Obamacare, which is the major area that Oscar Health operates - and in fact, disrupts. This healthcare policy reform has since been holding insurers back by limiting their profitability, increasing transparency over pricing and ultimately holding them accountable.

The government-level headwinds are obvious to investors, therefore the market is shying away from investing in this sector, which has been a stable and profitable industry before the current Republicans administration. As such, most, if not all, insurers are being priced at the lowest level in more than 3 years. Molina Health, which has been one of the most affected insurers, has expressed that a trough (of profitability) can only be seen in 2026 or even 2027, in its recent earnings.

However, for OSCR, we believe this is far too pessimistic and ignored where the real market is: demands for individual health insurance. CEO Mark Bertolini actually built the business based on the assumption of no ACA subsidy - hence no government policy is going to affect its path back to profitability.

Most importantly, from the recent 2025FY earnings, it shows that even without extended subsidies, the Company managed to continue to serve 3.4 million members as of February 2026, which is a far less decline than most would expected given the policy headwinds. Even in Q2 0.4 million would potentially not renew, and with a churn rate of 1-2% monthly subsequently, this represents about 2.7 million members by year end.

This impressive retention of members has once again shown that, while the individual health insurance marketplace was originated from national policies, the individual demands from freelance or part-time workers are what will drive the company further, not to mention that the Individual Coverage HRA (ICHRA) is still not taken into account yet - Oliver Wyman predicts it will at least grow at a 20% CAGR before 2029.

Technology: Converting business into profit, through AI and data

One thing that Oscar Health boasts is its technology - The Company is actually built for using technology to improve efficiency and enact cost-cutting that its peers cannot, while at the same time managed by a CEO that is "a hard-nosed businessman" and "doing anything for the bottom line", according to a New York Times article.

To an insurer, the most important ratios are: 1) Medical Loss Ratio (MLR), which is how much of the premium collected is claimed, and 2) SG&A, which represents the operating efficiency and customer acquisition.

Oscar Health has been consistently lowering its SG&A, to a 17.5% lower in 2025. This has been driven by technology and AI, which Mark mentioned that dozens of LLMs and Agentic AIs are going to be put to work, allowing customers "access quicker with much more accuracy and without having to wait on phones". This is where the impact of AI will be shown massively in real life, making money for companies.

On MLR, this is far from a company-specific performance metric. Rather, looking across all insurers, the medical claim ratio has peaked in 2025 for multiple reasons, like adverse selection (subscribers claim more because of more expensive insurance package) and higher medical cost. However, this is where technology will help as well - Oscar Health uses its "full-stack" technology internal platform and its customer-facing +Oscar suite to lower its MLR by improving member engagement, streamlining case handling and enhancing risk adjustment accuracy. Also, the increase in MLR will be offset by the Company's planned premium increase in 2026.

2026 guidance and valuation

Full Year 2026 Financial Outlook
