  • Total Revenue: Between $18.7 billion and $19.0 billion. This represents a YoY increase of more than 60% compared to $11.7 billion in 2025, driven by member growth, product improvements, and market share gains.
  • Medical Loss Ratio (MLR): 82.4% to 83.4%. This is a massive recovery from the 87.4% full-year MLR in 2025. The improvement also is a result of the 28% weighted average price increase for 2026.
  • SG&A Expense Ratio: 15.8% and 16.3%. Improve from 17.5% in 2025
  • Earnings from Operations: $250 million to $450 million. Finally, the management is forecasting a return to profitability, bouncing back from the operating loss of approximately $396 million in 2025.

Implications to valuation

With the guided range of earnings, earnings per share is projected to be:

The above is based on management guidance (which is usually under-promised based on track record) and a conservative industry multiple. We can see that even from the most conservative valuation, there is significant upside in a year's time compared to where the Company is being traded at. As such, we cannot be more optimistic on the stock - especially during a volatile market like 2026, such undervaluation opportunity is rare.

Falsifiable conditions: What will prove our thesis wrong?

To me, the forecast of 50%+ YoY jump in revenue is not something dramatic and not a problem to me, given the less-than-expected decline in members as discussed and the planned 28% increase in premium. So revenue guidance based on ~$7500 per member is solid.

However, profitability is still the most critical factor. Profitability is determined by the combined ratio of the below, which we need to pay attention to in the coming financial year:

  1. SG&A ratio: This is the most "controllable" metrics the management can have. With the revenue growth rate at more than 50%, SG&A is supposed to be lowered meaningfully, particularly with its strength in technology. If the SG&A target is missed, I will be doubtful to management execution and its core competitiveness
  2. MLR and earnings: Return to profitability in 2026 has been a target for CEO and OSCR for a while now. So any misstep in its path back to profitability is dangerous to its share price. Meanwhile, "profitability" means MLR + SG&A ratio < 100%. This indicates that MLR, be it industry-wise and company-specific trend, would be the key metric to watch and to decide whether one should adjust its position.

Other risks

  • Industry-wide volatility: MOH and UNH both experienced a significant 20%+ decline following latest earnings announcement. It shows that the fear level towards insurers is heightened, and any misstep could be disastrous. OSCR is not immune to such fear, even though fundamentally it is a good company. However, wisdom tells us "market can stay irrational longer than you can stay solvent"
  • Policy uncertainty: Current political landscape is uncertain; direction in the healthcare industry is unclear; bi-partisan support of health insurance policies is non-existent. Therefore, OSCR's execution is not totally in the management's control.

data.title: South Korea's Chip-Fueled Surge Is Compelling But Temporary
data.description:
The last year has been a fantastic time for investors with exposure to South Korea. Driven by excitement over the semiconductor space and related technologies, there has been a flurry of activity in that market. Major market indices are up significantly, outperforming the S&P 500 and even the NASDAQ. This follows years of underperformance driven by broader economic and demographic issues that the country faces. Over the next few years, there is a real opportunity for investors in the market to do incredibly well by banking on continued success for some of the leading industries in South Korea. But if you have an investment horizon that extends beyond the next 5 to 10 years, you should tread cautiously.

Investors are right to be excited

The last year has been more or less a miracle for those with exposure to South Korea’s economy. In the chart above, for instance, you can see how the iShares MSCI South Korea ETF (EWY) has performed over the last 12 months. This ETF is up an astounding 142.5%. By comparison, the S&P 500 is up only 15.9% while the NASDAQ is up 18.2%. For those not familiar, EWY is an ETF that gives investors exposure to large and mid sized companies in South Korea. It currently has 82 holdings in it. And the largest of these is none other than Samsung Electronics (SSNLF). Other major holdings include the who’s who of South Korean companies including Hyundai and KB Financial Group (KB).

This is exciting, but it is important that we take a step back and assess the situation over a longer investment horizon. This is because, if we look at just the past five years, for instance, we get a very different picture. In the chart above, you can see that it's up only 54.4% over this window of time. By comparison, the S&P 500 is up 90.7% while the NASDAQ is up 70.6%. This is evidence that the recent surge truly has been recent. And it begs the question of what exactly has caused this jump over the last 12 months.

To first understand this, we should understand a little bit about the largest components of that country's economy. And what better place to start than with imports and exports? In 2024, the country saw $721 billion worth of exports. That compares to the $621 billion of imports. Unlike economies that have largely gutted their manufacturing assets and shifted entirely toward services over the last few decades, South Korea is still very much a net exporter. This does not mean that the country doesn't have a large services base. In fact, around 60% of its GDP is service oriented. But for the US, that number is closer to 77%.

In 2024, integrated circuits accounted for the single largest export category for the country at $139 billion. That's up from the $119 billion that it was responsible for back in 2018 when exports were $622 billion and imports were $530 billion. Integrated circuits referred to microchips that are built ON Semiconductor materials like silicon. Examples include memory chips, logic chips, microcontrollers, and power management chips. Now, to be clear, South Korea is not the largest player in the semiconductor market. That spot belongs to Taiwan, which in 2023 was responsible for about 65% of the global semiconductor foundry market. But South Korea was still a massive player, with a 19.3% market share globally.

Other major exports include cars, refined petroleum products, passenger and cargo ships, and machines that are used specifically for the production of semiconductors. Although the country imports a lot of integrated circuits as well, about $58.1 billion worth in 2024, most of what it imports are unfinished goods. Examples would be crude petroleum, petroleum gas, refined petroleum, and the like. As the global semiconductor industry expands, we should expect countries like Taiwan and South Korea to be major beneficiaries.

Personally, I am becoming weary of the prospect of overinvestment in all things AI. I have talked about that in detail in other articles. But if analysts are to be believed, then we should expect continued massive growth here. According to PricewaterhouseCooper, for instance, in an analysis conducted in September of last year, the global semiconductor market was worth about $627 billion in 2024. By the year 2030 it should expand to more than $1 trillion in size. AI will certainly be a big driver here. But we should also see significant expansion when it comes to the automotive market and the home appliances space. When it comes to the automotive market specifically, there will be significant upside potential because of electric vehicles. I am less worried about a potential bubble in that market than I am AI. So that makes me feel at ease to some extent.

It is worth noting that other analysts are even more optimistic about the opportunity that exists in the semiconductor space. Take McKinsey & Company as an example. Earlier this year, they stated that it was worth about $775 billion in 2024. And they expect that it could reach between $1.5 trillion and $1.8 trillion by the end of this decade. Their base case is for expansion to $1.6 trillion as investments in AI, electric vehicles, and more, grow materially. Most of the expansion will involve the computing and data storage space, which is expected to grow to $460 billion in revenue by 2030. That would represent a 15% annual growth rate from 2024. But the wireless market and the automotive space will also be huge drivers, growing by 10% and 13% annually, respectively.

The government in South Korea does not want to let this opportunity slip through the cracks. Back in late 2024, for instance, the country rolled out $10 billion in loans dedicated to support their domestic chip industry. And even though there have been significant challenges when it comes to trade because of the US government insisting that other countries are taking advantage of it, the government of South Korea continues to push forward with capital injections into the space. In early 2025, for instance, the country said that it would expand its financial support package for the semiconductor industry to roughly $23 billion. That was up from the $18 billion spent a year earlier.

There still exists uncertainty when it comes to U.S. trade. Earlier this year, President Donald Trump announced 25% tariffs on certain advanced AI and high-performance chips. And the US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick warned that both South Korean and Taiwanese companies that do not make investments into US production might face tariffs of up to 100%. Although nothing is set in stone when it comes to South Korea specifically, in January, the Taiwanese government promised $250 billion in direct investments by its own technology companies, with promises for an additional $250 billion in credit provided for them to expand their production capacity here. But in recent days, the country has pushed back on the goal that the US government has for 40% of chip supply from Taiwan to be relocated to the US. It's possible that some sort of deal might occur regarding South Korea as well, but that is speculative at this time.

Issues remain

Conceptually, it makes sense for investors to be excited about the opportunity that exists in South Korean stocks. However, we should not ignore broader economic and demographic issues that will continue to plague the company. But first, we should touch on some positives regarding the nation. And these fall outside of trade.

From a purely economic perspective, South Korea has actually been a pretty solid country over the last several years. In fact, you could say that things have gone well over the last couple of decades. In the chart above, you can see, using constant currency figures, GDP and GDP per capita in South Korea. This includes projections through the year 2030. From 2025 through 2030, total GDP generated by South Korea should expand by about 4.1% per annum. And GDP per capita should grow by about 4.2%. From 1980 through 2030, overall growth for GDP will have averaged about 7.3% annually, while the per capita figures have been up about 6.7%.

This is great in and of itself. It is a testament to the strength of their overall economy. And as economic conditions continue to look up, we should anticipate a relatively strong consumer. Take the unemployment rate, as an example. In the chart above, you can see what it has been for the last several decades. In 2025, it was estimated to be about 3%. And if everything goes according to plan, it should dip down to about 2.8% by the end of the decade. These figures are lower than what the US has right now of about 4.3%.

The expectation that experts have right now is that, at least in the short run, real GDP growth should be expanding. After falling from 2% in 2024 to only 0.9% in 2025, it's expected to rise to 2.2% next year before pulling slightly back to 1.9% by the year 2030. When you add on top of this the other data points that we have talked about already, I don't see too much to complain about in the short run. One could argue, of course, that the debt to GDP ratio for the nation is worsening. That is most certainly true.

At the turn of this century, government debt amounted to 16.1% of GDP. By 2025, that number had ballooned to 53.4%. By the end of this decade, it should grow further to 64.3%. Government spending, driven in large part by an aging population whose worker base is going to find it increasingly challenging to financially cover for the older generations, has become a necessity. However, compared to the US, the country still looks very principled. For context, in 2025, government debt amounted to 125% of GDP here at home.

The biggest risk for investors at this point in time is demographic in nature. The fact of the matter is that South Korea has one of the lowest fertility rates in the world. In order to keep a population stable without resorting to immigration, country needs to have a fertility rate of about 2.1. Estimates vary, but according to one source, the fertility rate in South Korea is about 0.79. Changes in lifestyle preferences, rising living costs and wealth stratification, and other factors, can be blamed on this. And it's not something that's just happening in South Korea. It's happening in Japan, China, and even in the US. Historically, South Korea has been more resistant to the idea of immigration than the US has been. So the end result, despite government investments aimed at boosting the fertility rate, will be a drop in the overall population as time goes on.

We are not talking about a small change here either. South Korea's population peaked in 2020 at about 51.84 million. By 2024, it inched lower to 51.72 million. And in fact, that was actually the highest it had been in three years. But by the year 2050, it's forecasted to fall to 45.14 million. And if something doesn't change, it's going to drop further to 21.85 million by the year 2100. That's a decline of 57.8% in just 76 years. Although improvements in technology should boost productivity, at some point, productivity improvements cannot keep up. And when you add on top of this population declines projected for Japan, China, and other significant countries, there is a risk of an overall weakening for many of the industries that are important to the country, such as those involved in vehicle manufacturing.

One thing that is helping the country in the near term is that its currency, the won, is weakening compared to other currencies like the US dollar. This is helpful in boosting exports. But unfortunately, it does make imports more expensive. The good news is that since many of the imports that the country receives are unrefined materials that go into the goods that they export, the value-added nature of the production chain for the nation should allow it to still capture a lot of wealth, especially to the extent that the industries that it operates in are in high demand and offer fat margins.

Unfortunately, for investors who transact using US dollars, as well as currencies besides the won, continued weakening could prove problematic. A 10% increase in the value of stocks that are denominated in that country's local currency can be wiped out by a 10% drop in the value of the country. So the best opportunities will likely be for those who already transact in South Korea’s won. For those who are looking to invest in individual companies in South Korea, however, there is the potential of upside since many of its largest countries do transact using the US dollar. Even as their local currency weakens, you would expect the strengthening dollar to prove to be a boon for those businesses.

Coming into this article, I fully expected that the surge in the market in South Korea would have pushed market valuations well above what we have here in the US. That has not happened, though. Right now, for instance, the price-to-earnings multiple of EWY is 19. That's lower than the 23.6 for the S&P 500 and the 32.5 for the NASDAQ. The price-to-book multiple is 2.23. That compares to the 5.14 when it comes to the S&P 500 and to the 7.20 when it comes to the NASDAQ. The fact that it's still trading at a discount to these major US indices is evidence to me that upside potential exists. However, we should not ever expect parity. In addition to the fact that the composition of our economies is different, with the US focus on services leading to greater profit margins and lower cost growth, you also have the aforementioned demographic issues that will continue to plague the country for the rest of this century.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, it is my belief that South Korea could offer some nice upside potential over the next couple of years as investments in the semiconductor space continue to ratchet up. Investing in specific ETFs has been incredibly profitable for many market participants. Probably the best way to invest in the country if your goal is to pick individual stocks is to find those that transact using the dollar. But regardless of how you approach this, if you extend out beyond the next few years, the picture does become a lot riskier. This is because of the demographic time bomb that has already detonated in the country. For the next few years, it's not unthinkable that continued growth in key markets will push overall wealth for South Korea higher. But at some point, the shoe will be on the other foot. Prior to that stage, the goal should be to take a more cautious approach to the country.
